editor_settings.title: I Tried the RTX 4090 in 2026 So You Don’t Have To
editor_settings.content:
The GeForce RTX 4090 was a beast when it launched, and in 2026, it remains a monster. But the landscape has shifted. We are no longer just looking at a gaming card; we are looking at a piece of silicon that has been swept up in the AI gold rush, making its price history look like a mountain range and its "worth it" factor a moving target.

This GPU has a unique twist—can you figure it out?

😍 ROG Strix GeForce RTX 4090 BTF Edition @ASUS_ROG pic.twitter.com/XCUJOSSjVc

— Designs By IFR (@DesignsByIFR) May 21, 2024

Quick Verdict

The RTX 4090 is still the high water mark for reliable 4K performance, especially with software maturing to fill the hardware gaps. If you can find one at a sensible price, it is a fortress of a card, but buying retail in today’s inflated market is a hard pill to swallow.

Who It’s For / Who Should Skip

  • Buy it if: You are a professional creator, an AI researcher, or a 4K 144Hz purist who refuses to drop $3,500 on the latest flagship but still wants top tier performance.

  • Skip it if: You are gaming at 1440p or 1080p. Using a 4090 for anything less than 4K is like using a sledgehammer to crack a nut; you are literally leaving half the card's potential on the table.

  • [product-card:asus-geforce-rtx-4090-rog-strix-24gb-gddr6x-graphics-card]


Design & First Impressions

The first thing you notice about any 4090, particularly the partner cards, is the sheer scale. It is not a component; it is an appliance. The build quality on enthusiast models is meant to handle 450 watts of heat, which means massive fin stacks and vapor chambers that make it a 3.5 slot behemoth.


Specs That Actually Matter

  • 24GB GDDR6X VRAM: This is why the card is still relevant. For 8K textures and AI model training, anything less is a bottleneck.

  • DLSS 4.5 Support: While it launched with DLSS 3, software updates in 2026 have allowed this card to utilize newer AI upscaling techniques that keep frame rates competitive with newer generations.

  • Ada Lovelace Architecture: It remains remarkably efficient compared to the raw power draw of some 50-series counterparts.

Real-World Performance

In modern AAA titles, the 4090 still holds its own. Even with the release of the 50-series, the 4090 manages consistent 4K performance in heavy hitters like Alan Wake II and Cyberpunk 2077. The 24GB of memory ensures that you aren't seeing texture pop-in, even when pushing ultra-high resolution packs that would choke a lesser card.

@raptortechmodding Nvidia ASUS Strix RTX 4090 😍😍 @ASUS @rog_mx @NVIDIA GeForce @NVIDIA GeForce LATAM #4090 #rtx #rtx4090 #rog #strix #asus #asusrog #nvidia #nvidiageforce #gpu #tarjetadevideo #pcmasterrace ♬ sonido original - RaptorTech Modding PC


The Hype Factor

The 4090 is no longer just "the fast card." It is "the AI card." Because of its high VRAM and CUDA core count, it is in constant demand by researchers and developers, which has kept its status high and its availability low. It is a status symbol for both gamers and tech professionals alike.


Pricing Reality Check

The retail situation is, frankly, a mess. While the MSRP was originally $1,599, many "new" listings in 2026 are hovering between $2,800 and $3,500 due to AI-driven demand. It is a seller's market for brand-new units.

However, if you look beyond the obvious retail listings, the used market is where the logic lives. You can often find well-maintained Founders Edition or TUF models for $1,400 to $1,800. Given that these cards were built to last, a used 4090 at $1,500 is a significantly better value proposition than a brand new mid-range card at today's prices.

Comparison Section

  • GeForce RTX 5090: Around 27% faster but roughly 40% more expensive in the current retail climate. The 4090 offers better "bang for buck" if you aren't chasing the absolute bleeding edge.

  • Radeon RX 7900 XTX: A much cheaper alternative for pure rasterization, but it falls flat once you turn on heavy ray tracing or need CUDA for professional work.

  • GeForce RTX 4080 Super: Great for high-end gaming, but the 16GB VRAM limit makes it a "skip" for serious AI or 8K work.

Is It Worth It?

If you can secure one for under $1,800 on the secondary market, it is a resounding yes. It will likely remain a relevant 4K card for another three to four years. If you are looking at paying $3,000 for a new one at a retail outlet, you are better off waiting for the market to cool or looking at the next generation flagship.

Final Verdict

The GeForce RTX 4090 is a powerhouse that refused to die. It is the best "old" card you can buy, provided you don't let a retailer rob you at gunpoint for a "new" unit. Buy used, buy smart, and enjoy the 4K glory.
